text: The patient is a fifty-seven year old male. Seven months ago, he underwent Hartmann's operation due to a transanal low rectal penetrating injury caused by a steel beam that went through his anus and into the pelvic cavity. Operation findings showed rectal perforation along with mesenteric tearing of the sigmoid colon mesentery.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and after skin preparation and draping, an access port is created by deepening the colostomy into the abdominal cavity. A glove port is inserted, and the colonic limb is mobilized. Extensive adhesions to the abdominal wall and small bowel are observed, and sharp dissection is used to lyse the adhesions to the abdominal wall.

The surgeon inserts an additional 5mm trocar into the right lower abdomen, later used as a drain port. Meticulous dissection of the small bowel segment adhered to the colonic mesentery is performed using endoshears. After mobilizing the colon, the surgeon identifies the rectal stump and clears nearby adhesions with sharp dissection.

The surgeon inserts an anvil through the glove port and a circular stapler through the anus. The stapler penetrates the rectal mucosa, and docking is performed. The stapler is fired, completing the anastomosis. An air leakage test and intraoperative endoscopic evaluation are conducted to confirm the integrity of the anastomosis, showing no signs of leakage.

In summary, the operation lasted one hundred and eighty minutes. Expected blood loss was about one hundred cc's. The patient was given an oral diet on post-operative day six and was discharged on day nine without any complications.
